macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Bechor's Double Portion in Increased Property Value

micro_segments.title: Setting up the debate: when does a bechor receive double on post-mortem increases
Conceptual framework: double portion as a pre-existing stake vs. estate-wide increase
Tosafot's distinction: divided vs. undivided estate and timing of allocation

macro_segments.title: The Case of a Rented Cow and Natural Growth: Baraita and Gemara Analysis

micro_segments.title: Baraita: bechor receives double for a cow that gives birth, whether rented or grazing
Gemara's question: why distinguish rented from grazing if both involve increase
Key principle: automatic growth with no heirs' investment qualifies as pre-existing
Rashbam's explanation: orphans' non-investment and lack of feed costs determine status

macro_segments.title: Rabbi vs. Rabbanan: The Core Dispute on Natural Increase

micro_segments.title: Attribution: the baraita represents Rabbi's position allowing double on natural increase
Rabbi's principle: natural increase is deemed already in father's possession

macro_segments.title: Cases of Inheritance: Debts Owed and Debts Against the Estate

micro_segments.title: Case 1: estate inherits a debt (money owed to them); bechor takes double
Conceptual question: is accounts receivable considered possessed property or new income
Case 2: estate owes a debt; bechor pays double portion of creditor claim
Bechor's option to waive double portion if liability exceeds benefit
Rabbeinu Tam's alternative reading: personal debt against the bechor himself

macro_segments.title: Rabbi vs. Rabbanan: The Core Dispute on Natural Increase (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbanan's principle: only actual possessions transfer; increase requires full ownership
Verse interpretation: Rabbi equates increase to normal portions; Rabbanan view it as gift

macro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's Refinement: Degrees of Natural Growth and Transformation

micro_segments.title: Palm tree thickening and land enrichment: all agree bechor gets double on natural growth
Object transformation: sprouts becoming stalks or saplings becoming palms
Rabbi views transformation as natural; Rabbanan view it as new object

macro_segments.title: Varying Authorities on Psak Halacha: When Is Rabbi's Position Binding

micro_segments.title: Rabba bar bar Chana: no clear psak; both positions are legitimate
General principle inquiry: does halacha follow Rabbi against one colleague or many
Rav Nachman in name of Rav: cannot pasken like Rabbi (position rejected)
Rav Nachman independently: allowed to pasken like Rabbi

macro_segments.title: The Books of Beit Rav: Sources and Textual Tradition

micro_segments.title: Identifying the Sifrei and other Midrashei Halacha of the Rabbi Akiva school

macro_segments.title: Varying Authorities on Psak Halacha: When Is Rabbi's Position Binding (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava: prohibited to pasken like Rabbi but judgment not reversed if done

macro_segments.title: The Books of Beit Rav: Sources and Textual Tradition (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rashbam's bibliography: Sifra as Rabbi Yehuda, Sifrei as Rabbi Shimon
Definition of Debei Rav: either the school of Rav or the study hall itself

macro_segments.title: Debt Owed to the Estate: Rabbi and Rabbanan Positions

micro_segments.title: Braita: bechor receives double in inherited debt document (shtar chov)
Initial position (Rav Yehuda/Shmuel): bechor gets no double in loan or interest
Contradiction: braita shows bechor gets double including interest; must be Rabbi's view
Explanation: Rabbi deems loan principal and interest automatic increase like rent
Middle position (Shalchu mitam): bechor gets double on principal but not interest
Interpretation: different Rabbanan views on whether debt with document is possessed
